message:
refactor(runtime): rename runtime_compat:: -> runtime_async:: across production sources (ft-y378j.2)

Step 2 of the ft-y378j decomposition. Per
docs/proposals/ft-y378j-rename-decomposition.md, the rename of
runtime_compat to runtime_async previously landed in name only —
695+ inline references across 80 files in frankenterm-core/src
plus more across the sub-crates and the binary, against just one
file using the new name. The deprecated `pub use runtime_async as
runtime_compat;` alias in lib.rs preserves backward compat through
the migration window.

Mechanics:
  - Bulk Python sed: r"\bruntime_compat::" -> "runtime_async::"
  - Scope: all production .rs files under
      crates/frankenterm-core/src/
      crates/frankenterm-core-tantivy/src/
      crates/frankenterm-core-resource-types/src/
      crates/frankenterm-core-telemetry-types/src/
      crates/frankenterm-core-replay/src/
      crates/frankenterm/src/
      crates/frankenterm-core/benches/
  - 86 files modified, 901 substitutions total.
  - Bare `runtime_compat` mentions in comments / log strings (39
    sites) intentionally preserved — they are historical context and
    log-search patterns. Only path expressions (`runtime_compat::X`)
    were rewritten.
  - The `pub use crate::runtime_async as runtime_compat;` alias
    declaration in lib.rs is preserved unchanged (the substitution
    pattern requires `::`, which the alias line doesn't have).

Tests are out of scope for this commit (ft-y378j.1 owns the test
tree). The deprecation alias removal is ft-y378j.4 deferred to one
release after .3 ships.

Verified: cargo check -p frankenterm-core -p frankenterm-core-tantivy
-p frankenterm-core-replay -p frankenterm-core-ars clean.
0 remaining `runtime_compat::` references in
crates/frankenterm-core/src/.

Closes ft-y378j.2.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -496,9 +496,9 @@
 {"id":"ft-xydxz","title":"[review][LOW] ft-eze48 ftvi fuzz corpus has only one seed (FTVI magic + EOF), sparse coverage for libfuzzer starting points","description":"commit 6567397b landed fuzz/fuzz_targets/ftvi_index_from_bytes.rs with a single corpus seed at fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/short_magic. Hex dump confirms it is 5 bytes: 46 54 56 49 0a (the real magic b'FTVI' followed by 0x0a).

The seed uses the correct magic bytes (verified against crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:10 const MAGIC = b'FTVI'). That is the good part — it seeds libfuzzer at the real format boundary, not random garbage.

Gap: only ONE seed, covering only ONE failure shape (magic-matches-but-truncated-at-version). A properly-seeded fuzz target typically ships 5-10 seeds exercising:
- Empty input (zero bytes)
- Minimal valid FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=0 + count=0 (12 bytes total, no records)
- Single-record FTVI: magic + version=1 + dimension=1 + count=1 + one (u64 id, u16 f16-value) record
- Magic-only (4 bytes — truncates before version)
- Valid magic + valid version + truncated at dimension (6 bytes)
- Valid header claiming count=1 + zero record bytes (parser must not OOB)
- Valid header + bogus version (e.g., version=255) to exercise version rejection

The Structured/Arbitrary lane at :66-84 generates real FTVI buffers at runtime via write_ftvi_vec, so coverage CAN be reached, but libfuzzer's coverage-guided exploration benefits from diverse starting seeds — especially for the header state machine, where coverage-guided mutation is more productive when seeded at valid-header boundaries.

Fix: add 5-7 curated seed files to fuzz/corpus/ftvi_index_from_bytes/ constructed via write_ftvi_vec and dd/printf. One-time scripting cost.

Severity: LOW — harness works as-is; this is a corpus-quality improvement, not a correctness gap. Audit round 14, 2026-04-23.

Tag: review, fuzz, ftvi, corpus-quality.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:37:51.674698Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:52:07.939748Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:52:07.939424Z","close_reason":"Already shipped in 87afb28e: add four curated ftvi corpus seeds","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["review"]}
 {"id":"ft-y0loj","title":"[STRATEGIC/MEDIUM] frankenterm-core is a 488-file / ~854K-line monolith — every consumer pays full compile cost; feature-flag N×M matrix uncitable","description":"Modes-of-reasoning F5: lib.rs has 403 pub mod/mod in 538 lines. No sub-crates for MCP/connectors/ARS/patterns/mission/tx/fleet/native-mux.  {"id":"ft-y87lf","title":"pdu_stream_decode ASan allocation-size-too-big on new fuzz artifact","description":"`cargo +nightly fuzz run pdu_stream_decode --features codec-fuzz-targets -- -max_total_time=120 -detect_leaks=0` found a new crash in `codec::Pdu::stream_decode` under ASan.

Artifact: `fuzz/artifacts/pdu_stream_decode/crash-22974e1f96d936743995342b9a12dc33a8af8f51`
Repro: `cargo fuzz run --features=codec-fuzz-targets pdu_stream_decode fuzz/artifacts/pdu_stream_decode/crash-22974e1f96d936743995342b9a12dc33a8af8f51`

Observed failure: `AddressSanitizer: requested allocation size 0x1fffffd2000000 exceeds maximum supported size` followed by `SIGABRT`. This looks like a new oversized-allocation path despite the existing OOM regression coverage in `frankenterm/codec/tests/regression_oom_crasher.rs`.

The short soak also grew corpus coverage substantially before the abort; no other new artifacts were emitted during the run.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:36.123052Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:44:35.881131Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:44:35.880846Z","close_reason":"Bounded ClusteredLine and codec decode allocations; pinned the 22974e fuzz crasher in regression and corpus","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-y9lnb","title":"[EPIC] Transaction execution data-plane: connect orchestration to real pane operations, policy gates, and agent dispatch","description":"# Goal
Bridge the gap between FrankenTerm's well-engineered tx/mission orchestration framework and actual execution against live panes. Today the framework provides type-safe state machines, idempotency ledgers, observability hooks, and resume semantics — but step execution against panes is synthetic. This epic wires the data plane so that ft tx run actually does things.

# Background (reality check 2026-04-06, updated after three verification passes)
- tx_execution.rs (1,663 lines, 28 tests): StepExecutor trait with 3 methods. SyntheticStepExecutor for testing, PolicyPrepareStepExecutor for real gate evaluation (DONE). TxExecutionEngine<E: StepExecutor> orchestrates full lifecycle but is NOT used by the CLI.
- plan.rs (7,760 lines, 123 tests): Core types solid. Gate evaluation real via TxPreparePolicyAuthorizer/TxPrepareApprovalChecker/TxPrepareTargetLookup traits with StorageBacked impls. StepAction enum (line 529) has SendText, WaitFor, AcquireLock, etc.
- tx_idempotency.rs (2,041 lines, 68 tests): Strong implementation. Integrated in TxExecutionEngine.
- mission_loop.rs (5,291 lines, 116 tests): Real evaluation loop but dispatch is not connected.
- tx_observability.rs (1,287 lines, 20 tests): Event taxonomy complete, integrated in TxExecutionEngine.

# Critical CLI Finding (pass 3)
Both ft tx run AND ft robot tx run import:
  tx_prepare_gate_inputs_allow_all as build_robot_tx_prepare_gate_inputs
This means the CLI BYPASSES all policy gates despite SnowyHorse's PolicyPrepareStepExecutor work. The CLI also doesn't use TxExecutionEngine — it manually calls evaluate_prepare_phase / execute_commit_phase with synthetic inputs. The ft-y9lnb.6 bead addresses this.

# What's Done
- Policy gate wiring (ft-y9lnb.2, closed): prepare-phase traits implemented with StorageBacked adapters.

# What Remains
1. PaneStepExecutor: real execute_steps() and execute_compensations() (ft-y9lnb.1)
2. Mission dispatch: MissionDispatchContract -> WorkflowRunner bridge (ft-y9lnb.3)
3. Timeout/backpressure: per-step/phase/tx timeouts + FleetPressureTier (ft-y9lnb.4)
4. Integration tests: 18 scenarios via TxExecutionEngine<PaneStepExecutor> (ft-y9lnb.5)
5. CLI wiring: ft tx run/rollback use TxExecutionEngine + PaneStepExecutor, add --dry-run (ft-y9lnb.6)

# Acceptance Criteria
1. ft tx run executes real pane operations via PaneStepExecutor, not synthetic stubs.
2. Both human CLI and Robot Mode use real policy gates (no more allow_all import).
3. ft tx rollback sends real compensation commands.
4. ft tx run --dry-run validates permissions without executing (new user safety feature).
5. TxExecutionEngine orchestrates the lifecycle (not manual phase calls in main.rs).
6. Every step produces TxObservabilityEvent audit trail + idempotency ledger entries.
7. 18+ integration tests + 3 E2E scenarios prove the full pipeline.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:53:12.486800Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:58:22.449058Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:58:22.448822Z","close_reason":"All 6 child beads closed: .1 PaneStepExecutor, .2 policy gates, .3 mission dispatch, .4 timeouts/backpressure, .5 integration tests (30 tests), .6 CLI wiring. The tx data-plane is fully wired: ft tx run/rollback uses real PaneStepExecutor with policy gates, timeouts, backpressure, and falls back to SyntheticStepExecutor when backend unavailable.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["data-plane","goal-line","mission","tx"]}
